name: From Lumbrales to the Duero River - Arribes del Duero Natural Park — circular via Hinojosa de Duero
name_original: De Lumbrales al Río Duero - Parque Natural de Arribes del Duero — circular por Hinojosa de Duero
region_intro: Road cycling around Lumbrales offers diverse terrain within the Arribes del Duero Natural Park, characterized by dramatic canyons and gorges carved by the Duero River and its tributaries. The region features a mix of landscapes, from river valleys and traditional dehesa to agricultural plains, providing varied scenery for cyclists. Roads are generally well-paved, leading through partly hilly terrain with options for both moderate and substantial elevation gains. This environment supports a range of road cycling experiences, from scenic routes along the river to challenging climbs. Best road cycling routes around Lumbrales The most popular road cycling route is From Lumbrales to the Duero River - Arribes del Duero Natural Park — circular via Hinojosa de Duero, a 33.0 miles (53.0 km) trail that takes 2 hours 50 minutes to complete. This route offers direct access to the park's scenic beauty, winding through canyons and past traditional villages. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Arribes del Duero International Park Loop, a difficult 59.4 miles (95.6 km) path. This route provides expansive views of the international park and features substantial elevation changes. Local road cyclists also love the Lumbrales – Hinojosa de Duero loop from Somos lo que damos, a 50.8 miles (81.7 km) trail leading through varied scenery and traditional villages, often completed in about 3 hours 56 minutes. Road cycling around Lumbrales is defined by dramatic canyons, river valleys, and traditional dehesa. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easier sections to routes with difficult ascents. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 190 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Lumbrales's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Lumbrales?
A: There are over 25 road cycling routes available around Lumbrales, offering a variety of experiences within the Arribes del Duero Natural Park. These routes cater to different skill levels, from moderate to difficult.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling in Lumbrales?
A: The terrain around Lumbrales is diverse, characterized by dramatic canyons and gorges carved by the Duero River and its tributaries. You'll find a mix of river valleys, traditional &lt;em&gt;dehesa&lt;/em&gt; (holm oak meadows), and agricultural plains. Roads are generally well-paved, leading through partly hilly terrain with options for both moderate and substantial elevation gains, including challenging ascents within the Arribes del Duero.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in Lumbrales?
A: While many routes in Lumbrales are rated as difficult due to significant elevation changes, there are some easier sections and routes available. For instance, the region offers routes with 'mostly well-paved surfaces and easy to ride' sections and moderate elevation gains, which can be suitable for less experienced cyclists or families looking for a more relaxed ride.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes for experienced cyclists in Lumbrales?
A: Yes, Lumbrales is an excellent destination for experienced cyclists seeking a challenge. The region offers routes with 'substantial elevation' and 'difficult ascents,' particularly within the mountainous uplands and ravines of the Arribes del Duero. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1026402573/arribes-del-duero-international-park-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arribes del Duero International Park Loop&lt;/a&gt;, covering nearly 96 km with over 1800 meters of elevation gain, provide a demanding experience.

Q: What natural viewpoints can I visit while road cycling in the Lumbrales area?
A: The Arribes del Duero Natural Park is home to numerous breathtaking natural viewpoints that offer stunning vistas of the Duero River and the expansive natural park. Notable viewpoints include Peña La Vela, Mirador del Contrabando, Cachón del Camaces, Cachón de la Diabla, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5334730" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Saucelle Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;. These make for excellent rest stops and photo opportunities along your cycling routes.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural landmarks to see along the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, the Lumbrales area is rich in history and culture. You can cycle past the ruins of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5335377" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castro Vetón de Las Merchanas&lt;/a&gt;, an ancient pre-Roman settlement. Charming villages like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5441803" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hinojosa de Duero&lt;/a&gt; offer traditional architecture, and you might even spot the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4321091" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of San Felices de los Gallegos&lt;/a&gt;. Lumbrales itself has an Ethnographic Museum showcasing local life.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes in Lumbrales?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes around Lumbrales are designed as circular trips, allowing you to start and end in the same location. An example is the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1026394461/from-lumbrales-to-the-duero-river-arribes-del-duero-natural-park-circular-via-hinojosa-de-duero" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;From Lumbrales to the Duero River - Arribes del Duero Natural Park — circular via Hinojosa de Duero&lt;/a&gt;, which offers direct access to the park's scenic beauty.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Lumbrales?
A: The region's climate makes spring and autumn ideal for road cycling in Lumbrales. During these seasons, temperatures are generally milder, and the natural landscapes are particularly vibrant. Summers can be hot, while winters might bring cooler temperatures, so checking the weather forecast is always recommended.

Q: Is parking available for cyclists in Lumbrales?
A: Yes, Lumbrales, being a hub for outdoor activities, typically offers various parking options within the town. You can usually find public parking areas suitable for leaving your vehicle while you embark on your cycling adventure. Specific parking details might vary, so it's advisable to check local signage upon arrival.

Q: Are there bike repair facilities in or near Lumbrales?
A: While Lumbrales is a smaller town, it's a good practice to carry essential repair kits for minor issues. For more significant repairs, it's recommended to inquire locally upon arrival or check in larger nearby towns in the Salamanca province, as dedicated bike repair shops might be limited in very rural areas.

Q: Where can cyclists find places to eat or stay in Lumbrales?
A: Lumbrales offers various amenities for visitors, including cafes, restaurants, and accommodation options. You can find places to refuel with local cuisine or stay overnight, making it a convenient base for multi-day cycling trips. Many establishments are accustomed to catering to outdoor enthusiasts.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in Lumbrales?
A: Over 190 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Lumbrales's varied terrain. They often praise the region for its picturesque beauty, winding through stunning canyons and traditional villages, and the low traffic conditions on many roads, which significantly enhances the safety and enjoyment of road cycling.
tips: The Molinera bridge crosses the Huebra river at the bottom of the valley, at the same height as the port of the same name, 280 meters high.

Hill from where you can see the entire valley of the Huebra River until it reaches the Duero River. With a road bike it is difficult to go, but above the top of La Jana there is a viewpoint for a full view. But, from the road itself, it is possible to see incredible views of the valley.
Small viewpoint on the way to the Saucelle dam, you can see the town of Salto de Saucelle, now converted into a hotel complex. It has a free-access telescope to observe the landscape.

Hinojosa de Duero is a charming village in the El Abadengo region (Salamanca), located right on the border with Portugal and within the Arribes del Duero Natural Park, making it an ideal destination for rural tourism, nature and gastronomy.
